description: Apollo 7 Flight Journal - Day 1, part 2: CSM/S-IVB Orbital Operations

tokens: Walter, I'll get the [SPS] gimbals Off.
transcript.speaker: Schirra

tokens: Pitch 1, Off; Yaw 1, Off; Pitch 2, Off; Yaw 2, Off.
transcript.speaker: Eisele

tokens: All four Off.
transcript.speaker: Schirra

tokens: Beautiful.
transcript.speaker: Cunningham

tokens: Donn Eisele reads off the post insertion checklist steps as the crew begins to configure the spacecraft for orbital operations.
Wally Schirra switches the SPS Gimbal Motors off in sequence while Walt Cunningham confirms the DC Amps reading drops with each switch throw.
The drop in DC current confirms the motors are no longer operating.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: Roger.
Confirm DSKY readouts are velocity 25553; H-dot is minus four balls 1; and altitude is 122.3.
[Pause.]
transcript.speaker: Eisele

tokens: The values Eisele reads off were computed by the guidance computer running in Program 11.
The computer shows them traveling at 25,553 feet per second [7,789 m/s], descending at one foot per second [-0.3 m/s], at an altitude of 123.3 nautical miles [228.4 km].
These serve as a confirmation of good booster performance.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: Roger.
Copy, Apollo 7.
We have you Go for orbit, Go for orbit.
[Pause.]
transcript.speaker: Swigert

tokens: Apollo 7, your S-IVB has been safed.
[Pause.]
transcript.speaker: Swigert

tokens: And the Control Center has relayed word here, we show you Go for orbit.

For the first six revolutions around the Earth, it will be a revolution by revolution consideration, but these cut-off parameters were just nearly as perfect as they could be from the looks of our plot boards.
11 minutes, 30 seconds and the crew has been advised that the S-IVB systems have been safed.
The electrical circuits are shut down and the booster is considered in a - a quite safe configuration.
Some residual liquid hydrogen and liquid oxygen remaining aboard, that will be vented as we move through this first revolution.
transcript.speaker: PAO

tokens: Initial safing of the S-IVB stage started at engine cut-off.
The liquid hydrogen (LH2) tank passivation valve opened and a non-propulsive LH2vent was started.
About 30 seconds later, a short liquid oxygen (LOX) vent was performed.
These vents served to keep the LH2and LOX tank pressures within acceptable limits until a final propellant dump near the end of the first orbit.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: Apollo 7, Houston.
Are you reading?
[Pause.]
transcript.speaker: Swigert

tokens: Apollo 7, Houston.
transcript.speaker: Swigert

tokens: Let me read these off: apogee 146.4, perigee 122.3 off the DSKY.
transcript.speaker: Eisele

tokens: Apollo 7, Houston.
We copy your perigee and apogee.
transcript.speaker: Swigert

tokens: Eisele has keyed Verb 82 into the DSKY and reads off the perigee and apogee calculated by the onboard computer.
The computer shows them in an elliptical orbit with a low point of 122.3 nautical miles [226.5 km] and highest point of 146.4 nautical miles [271.1 km].
The orbit computed just after insertion of usually off somewhat from the actual orbit.
A more accurate calculation is being made on the ground and will be relayed up to the crew.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: How are you reading Houston?
[Long Pause.]
transcript.speaker: Swigert

tokens: Apollo 7, Houston.
Omni Delta, omni Delta.
[Long Pause.]
transcript.speaker: Swigert

tokens: Apollo 7, Houston.
How are you reading?
transcript.speaker: Swigert

tokens: Read you loud and clear, Houston.
How are we?
transcript.speaker: Crewman

tokens: You're five-by now, 7.
Your S-IVB has been safed.
Stand by for your orbit.
transcript.speaker: Swigert

tokens: Very good, sir.
transcript.speaker: Schirra

tokens: Apollo 7, Houston.
We have you in a 122 by 151 orbit.
transcript.speaker: Swigert

tokens: This orbit of 122 nautical miles [226 km] by 151 nautical miles [280 km] was computed using data from the Bermuda tracking station.
A later compilation of all available data would show the actual orbit at insertion as 123.3 by 153.7 nautical miles [228.4 by 284.7 km].
This is only slightly higher than the planned orbit of 123 by 151.5 nautical miles [227.8 by 280.6 km].
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: This is Apollo Control in Houston at 13 minutes into the flight.
We are still getting excellent communications from Apollo 7 which is now in the middle of the Atlantic Ocean.
The quality of the voice comm is not that what it was in the first few seconds, but it - the first few minutes - but it is still quite readable.
transcript.speaker: PAO

tokens: That's very good.
transcript.speaker: Schirra

tokens: Not bad shooting, right?
transcript.speaker: Swigert

tokens: That's great.
transcript.speaker: Schirra

tokens: Comm break.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: Apollo 7, Houston.
[Long Pause.]
transcript.speaker: Swigert

tokens: Apollo 7, Houston.
[Long Pause.]
transcript.speaker: Swigert

tokens: Apollo 7, Houston.
[Long Pause.]
transcript.speaker: Swigert

tokens: Apollo 7, Houston.
transcript.speaker: Swigert

tokens: Okay.
I'll give you a GET [Ground Elapsed Time] time hack at 17 minutes in about 20 seconds.
transcript.speaker: Swigert

tokens: This is one of many time hacks given throughout the mission.
These are used to ensure the timers used by the crew and the ground are in sync or to start a countdown to a critical event.
In this case, Jack Swigert counts down to 17 minutes elapsed time using the timer in Mission Control.
Wally Schirra verifies that the timer onboard the spacecraft reads 17 minutes when Jack says "Mark."
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: Five, four, three, two, one.
transcript.speaker: Swigert

tokens: Mark.
17 minutes GET.
transcript.speaker: Swigert

tokens: Roger.
[Garbled]
transcript.speaker: Schirra

tokens: Roger.
You won't need a CMC lift-off update.
You're okay there.
transcript.speaker: Swigert

tokens: A CMC (Command Module Computer) lift-off time update could be uplinked to the onboard computer from the ground if any discrepancy existed between the computer's timer and timers on the ground.

A close synchronization between the two was necessary to allow accurate execution of time sensitive computer tasks such as control of SPS burns, or anywhere the task starts at a specific time after lift-off.
Any discrepancy between the time used on the ground to make the computations and the time used onboard to execute the task would lead to inaccuracies.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: Roger.
[Garble].
transcript.speaker: Schirra

tokens: Roger.
How are we transmitting?
transcript.speaker: Schirra

tokens: Okay.
We're reading you about two-by; we're really trying to do some reconfiguring here to get good comm with you.
transcript.speaker: Swigert

tokens: [Garble].
transcript.speaker: Schirra

tokens: I can't make it out, Wally.
Stand by.
transcript.speaker: Swigert

tokens: Roger.
[Long Pause.]
transcript.speaker: Schirra

tokens: Apollo 7, Houston.
How are you reading now?
transcript.speaker: Swigert

tokens: [Garble].
transcript.speaker: Schirra

tokens: Okay.
You're loud, but very garbled, Wally.
transcript.speaker: Swigert

tokens: Roger.
[Pause.]
transcript.speaker: Schirra

tokens: All your systems look real good down here.
transcript.speaker: Swigert

tokens: Roger.
[Garble].
transcript.speaker: Schirra

tokens: I couldn't make it out.
Do you want to select Simplex A?
[Long Pause.]
transcript.speaker: Swigert

tokens: Communications would clear up with selection of the Simplex A VHF mode by the crew.
The Canary Islands tracking station is being used at this point in the orbit.
This station had similar problems with the VHF receiver equipment as those experienced by the Bermuda station.]
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: That's land out there, little island down there that way.
Can you see it?
Walt can, I guess.
transcript.speaker: Schirra

tokens: Okay.
Apollo 7, Houston.
How do you read now?
transcript.speaker: Swigert

tokens: Houston, Apollo 7.
How do you read on Simplex A?
transcript.speaker: Schirra

tokens: You're real fine now, real fine, Wally, and we've got you coming through on intercom.
transcript.speaker: Swigert

tokens: That's clever.
transcript.speaker: Schirra

tokens: Okay.
transcript.speaker: Swigert

tokens: No, we're broadcasting to you.
transcript.speaker: Schirra

tokens: Oh, okay.
I was just wondering.
I couldn't see what you were describing there.
transcript.speaker: Swigert

tokens: We're looking at the Canary Islands.
transcript.speaker: Schirra

tokens: Oh, you're making me jealous.
transcript.speaker: Swigert

tokens: Roger.
We've completed the insertion checklist with the exception of the four circuit breakers on panels 277 and 278.
transcript.speaker: Schirra

tokens: Panel 277 and 278 are behind and to the right of Walt Cunningham in the LMP seat.
These will not be in reach until the crew unstraps and can move around more freely.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: Roger.
We copy.
transcript.speaker: Swigert

tokens: He hasn't posted this yet.
transcript.speaker: Schirra

tokens: It's loud and clear over here, Jack; good weather report.
transcript.speaker: Cunningham

tokens: Roger.
You're five-by also.
transcript.speaker: Swigert

tokens: Just deserts.
transcript.speaker: Schirra

tokens: This is Apollo Control in Houston, 21 minutes into the flight.

The ground and the crew finally got to the right antenna with the spacecraft over the Canary Islands and until that point, the comm from approximately mid-Atlantic on into the Canary area was pretty rocky - pretty rough and they went to one of the antennas which apparently was pointed in the proper direction and the comm cleared up markedly as you will be able to observe as we play this voice tape.
I've checked with the Flight Director; he tells me that Schirra's heart rate at lift-off was 87 beats per minute - 87 beats per minute at lift-off.
And at staging, when the first stage burned out and the second stage ignited, it hit its high point during the entire mission of 100 beats per minute.
One hundred beats at staging, 87 at lift-off; which is, I can never recall a lift-off heart rate in the 80s, I can recall some in the 90s and I think they were Wally's.
transcript.speaker: PAO

tokens: Loss Of Signal (LOS) occurs when the tracking station providing communication coverage passes under the horizon as seen from the spacecraft.
The stations are spread out around the world and only provide partial coverage as the spacecraft passes overhead.
The blank spaces in coverage are filled by recording onboard voice and systems data on an onboard tape and downlinking the recording to stations configured to receive the data.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: Very good; they feel good.
transcript.speaker: Schirra

tokens: Roger, Boss.
[Long Pause.]
transcript.speaker: Swigert

tokens: Apollo 7, Houston.
Can you give us an onboard reading of the S-IVB tank pressures?
transcript.speaker: Swigert

tokens: Roger.
Oxidizer is at 21[psi]; fuel is about 7[psi].
transcript.speaker: Schirra

tokens: Roger.
We confirm that, Wally.
Looks about the same here.
transcript.speaker: Swigert

tokens: Roger.
transcript.speaker: Schirra

tokens: Very Long Comm Break.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: The S-IVB tank pressures are read on the spacecraft off a panel on the left forward console.
The ground also has the capability to monitor the pressures through telemetry data.
Tank pressures of less than 50 psi were considered acceptable at this point in the mission.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: The onboard voice recording picks up again at this point.

The crew, aware that their conversations are being recorded, will use this capability to describe certain events and record readings from certain meters without having to write all the information down.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: This is Apollo Control in Houston with 25 minutes into the flight.
The crew, after a very brief 20-minute flight across the Atlantic Ocean, is now going across the African Continent.
We've lost touch with them as they move across Africa.
The official launch time was 15:02:45 Zulu time or Greenwich Mean Time, 15:02:45.

Our present orbital parameters are 122 by 151, 122 nautical miles by 151 nautical miles [226 by 280 km]; and we now begin to settle down to look at the business of the Flight Plan and one of the first events after leaving the Canary zone is to close the liquid hydrogen vent and it should be closed very shortly.
At Tananarive, the spacecraft will go into it's first sunset.
We will be talking to them through Tananarive.
The Tananarive station should acquire along about 36 minutes into the flight.
We're presently 26 minutes into the flight.
This is Apollo Control Houston.
transcript.speaker: PAO

tokens: A "swizzle stick" refers to either an extension attached to a switch to make it easier to reach or, in this case, a stick that could be held by the crew to provide additional reach.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: Walt is reading from the ECS Post-insertion checklist while Wally and Donn perform the steps.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: Walt is referring to starting the water/glycol fluid flow to the radiators as part of the ECS post-insertion configuration.
This would need to be done no earlier than 22 minutes into the flight but no later than 55 minutes into the flight.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: Although Wally Schirra was a veteran of two previous space flights, it is his first experience with moving around unrestrained on zero-g.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: A "talk-back" is referring to an indicator that will either be grey or barber-poled (striped) depending on the configuration of the system it is monitoring.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: This is Apollo Control in Houston at 36 minutes into the flight.

In the last minute or two we have reviewed the g data recorded on this flight and the curve is a very gentle one on the first stage, and it peaked right at about 4 g's, and of course it falls back to zero g for a short time and then it begins a gentle climb again and at the second stage shut down, some 10 minutes and 20 seconds into the flight, it was peaked out on the second stage at about 2½ g's.
We heard the crew remark at least once, perhaps several times, about what a gentle ride it was.
Glynn Lunney, our Flight Director, has gone around the room console by console.
He opened that session with these words.
"Okay, everybody, lets - we've got a long way to go.
Let's sit down and look at what we've got." He then went through each system and questioned each man about his particular specialty area during the launch phase and he got no surprises.
We have tagged up with the crew now over Tananarive.
We've put in a call, and at this point we've not heard from them, but Schirra should be installing an alignment sight in his window.
This will help him - it is something like a boresight - it will help him do a number of tasks and he will use it to look at all sorts of objects during the flight.

Very shortly we should open the liquid hydrogen vent valve and thin out some of the residual fuel in there in the liquid hydrogen tank, and over - that will take place over Carnarvon some 54 minutes into the flight.
They say the call went in by the Tananarive station about two minutes ago.
We've not heard from the crew.
We are getting a rather steady carrier noise on the line.
We'll standby and come up with that communication as it develops.
transcript.speaker: PAO

tokens: Hello.
transcript.speaker: Stafford

tokens: Apollo 7, Houston.
How do you read?
transcript.speaker: Stafford

tokens: Houston Capcom, Apollo 7 over Tananarive.
Do you read?
transcript.speaker: Schirra

tokens: Roger, Wally.
We're reading you loud and clear.
How do you read me?
transcript.speaker: Swigert

tokens: Very long comm break.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: Apollo Control here at 42 minutes into the flight.
Tom Stafford, one of our capsule communicators, has been running an HF voice communication check.

We heard from Apollo 7 loud and clear, but they apparently can not hear us, and we're going to continue this voice check by HF, which is the hard way, of course, and let's listen now as Stafford tries to contact the spacecraft.
transcript.speaker: PAO

tokens: Apollo Control here again.
We're still attempting to establish HF communications.

We want to emphasize that there is nothing wrong at all with our VHF channel via the other means of communication, but we are exploring just how good the HF system is at this extraordinarily long range.
Apparently the communicator at the Tananarive station may be hearing something from the spacecraft we are not.
It is just a shake down test of the total communications capability that we have, and we'll leave the carrier up, although it looks like they are moving out of the range of Tananarive.
We'll leave it up another minute or two.
At 44 minutes into the flight, the crew getting its first look at the Indian Ocean after departing the African continent.
This is Apollo Control, Houston.
transcript.speaker: PAO

tokens: There it is.
There it is now about halfway between the two layers.
transcript.speaker: Eisele

tokens: And this is Apollo Control Houston at 52 minutes into the flight.
In a very few seconds, we will put in a call to the crew by the Carnarvon station on the west coast of Australia.
I just got a call that Carnarvon had acquired - the acquisition aid had locked up.
The discussion over the Australian pass is always an interesting one and here comes the comm now.
Tom Stafford from here.
transcript.speaker: PAO

tokens: Houston CapCom, Apollo 7 over Carnarvon.
transcript.speaker: Schirra

tokens: Roger, Apollo 7, this is Houston.
Reading you loud and clear.
transcript.speaker: Stafford

tokens: Roger, read you same.
We're having a ball.
transcript.speaker: Cunningham

tokens: Roger.
We read you loud and clear over Tananarive, Wally, but evidently you could not read us.
transcript.speaker: Stafford

tokens: Fine, Tom.
transcript.speaker: Schirra

tokens: Okay.
We have new time for your LOX dump.
The LOX dump...
transcript.speaker: Stafford

tokens: Wait a minute.
Okay.
Go ahead, Tom.
transcript.speaker: Schirra

tokens: Roger.
The S-IVB dump will occur at 1 plus 34 plus 27, estimated Delta-V of 32 feet per second [10 m/s].
[Long Pause]
transcript.speaker: Stafford

tokens: Did you get that, Walt?
transcript.speaker: Schirra

tokens: Roger.
1 plus 34 plus 27, 32 feet per second.
transcript.speaker: Cunningham

tokens: Roger.
transcript.speaker: Stafford

tokens: Do you read?
transcript.speaker: Schirra

tokens: Roger.
We got them.
transcript.speaker: Stafford

tokens: Okay.
We've completed the post-insertion checklist down to where the CMP has to get out of the couch.
Standing by for your Go/No-Go.
transcript.speaker: Schirra

tokens: Roger.
transcript.speaker: Stafford

tokens: I'd like to give you a little fast report on what we get here.
transcript.speaker: Schirra

tokens: Go.
transcript.speaker: Stafford

tokens: The windows appear to be almost crystal clear - which is good news for all of us - and we have very good visibility out of all five windows.
And in that center hatch one, there is a drain for monitoring boost.
transcript.speaker: Schirra

tokens: Roger.
transcript.speaker: Stafford

tokens: We've noted the airglow here and made some data on it.
It looks like it's about 3 degrees thick as we approached Carnarvon - at night, of course.
We measured that with a COAS.
transcript.speaker: Schirra

tokens: Roger.
transcript.speaker: Stafford

tokens: [Garbled] POUs are still at 12 o'clock, [garble] arrived at 12 o'clock this trip.
transcript.speaker: Schirra

tokens: You've seen me before.
transcript.speaker: Stafford

tokens: (Laughter) Roger.
[Garble] came into view 3 degrees before the top of the airglow, where that was the surface of the Earth.
transcript.speaker: Schirra

tokens: Okay.
transcript.speaker: Stafford

tokens: I'll see if Donn and Walt have anything to pass on.
transcript.speaker: Schirra

tokens: Okay.
Stand by.
May want to get you a Nav load right now for the Go/No-Go.
Stand by.
transcript.speaker: Stafford

tokens: Okay.
[Long Pause.]
transcript.speaker: Schirra

tokens: Apollo 7, Houston.
transcript.speaker: Swigert

tokens: Go ahead.
transcript.speaker: Schirra

tokens: Roger.
You have a Go, and guidance would like to send you an update.
transcript.speaker: Swigert

tokens: Roger.
Stand by.
transcript.speaker: Schirra

tokens: Roger.
We're in Block; will go to Accept on your call.
transcript.speaker: Schirra

tokens: Roger.
Go to Accept.
transcript.speaker: Swigert

tokens: We are in Accept, Understand we're Go for two one.
transcript.speaker: Schirra

tokens: Affirmative.
transcript.speaker: Swigert

tokens: Roger.
Jack, I'm observing your rewinded tape dump.
We would like to get a good reading on Go/No-Go on the DSE as soon as you can.
transcript.speaker: Schirra

tokens: This is Apollo Control Houston.
The platform in the guidance component of the spacecraft checked out very nicely.
It's within less than a half of a degree in all respects, precisely where it should be.
The angles have all been taken into consideration now.
As we move across Australia, we would anticipate from the crew that they had begun to remove things, like gloves and their lifevests, and probably their helmets.
The Lunar Module Pilot Walt Cunningham, should be finishing up his insertion checklist, his extensive list.
transcript.speaker: PAO

tokens: Okay.
transcript.speaker: Swigert

tokens: Total of LOX dump was 1 plus 31 plus 27, 33 feet per second.
transcript.speaker: Schirra

tokens: Roger.
We copy.
Okay.
It's coming up.
[Long Pause.]
transcript.speaker: Swigert

tokens: Apollo 7, Houston.
The load is in, has been verified; the computer is yours.
transcript.speaker: Swigert

tokens: Very good.
[Long Pause.]
transcript.speaker: Schirra

tokens: Apollo 7, Houston.
One minute LOS Carnarvon; we pick you up Honeysuckle S-band almost immediately.
transcript.speaker: Swigert

tokens: Okay.
Jack, Donn is taking off his suit now; Walt's and mine are still on.
We get an O2Flow Hi when Donn opens up the suit, and we analyze that as the suit rate trying to catch up to the cabin, so we are Go.
transcript.speaker: Schirra

tokens: Okay.
We copy.
transcript.speaker: Swigert

tokens: Okay.
Jack, we're got the suit flow valve off now, and the O2flow is dropping down.
transcript.speaker: Schirra

tokens: Okay.
We copy.
transcript.speaker: Swigert

tokens: No problem; it's just that we haven't seemed to be able to stop at the right thing.
transcript.speaker: Schirra

tokens: Hey, Jack, are they going to be able to get the tape recorder rewound before we get LOS?
transcript.speaker: Cunningham

tokens: Stand by.
transcript.speaker: Swigert

tokens: [Garble] for 6 minutes.
transcript.speaker: Schirra

tokens: Okay.
Apollo 7, Houston.
We got the tape recorder rewound over the Canaries.
We will do a dump over MILA.
transcript.speaker: Swigert

tokens: Roger.
[Pause.]
transcript.speaker: Schirra

tokens: We would like to have a reading of just what you got on that tape, because we were talking on it continuously.
transcript.speaker: Cunningham

tokens: Okay.
Will do.
We'll do that over the stateside pass, Walt.
transcript.speaker: Swigert

tokens: Very long comm break.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: This is Apollo Control Houston.
We have lost signal via the Honeysuckle Creek outside of Canberra, Australia.

It is a new station to join our network, and we should not hear from the crew again until the ship Huntsville, parked half way between Hawaii and the coast of California, picks them up at 1 hour and 24 minutes into the flight.
We are 1 hour and 7 minutes into the flight right now and that was not a particularly communicative pass.
We had a little comm in the early part.
Apparently the crew going through their initial stowage of such things as the helmets and gloves.
They're getting out pieces of equipment and getting squared away for the early revs of the flight.
The stowage list called for the Command Pilot to stow various temperature devices, flight data files, install a urine filter, and adjust the couch position.
They will be also unstowing and assembling some cameras, and loading them with film as they fly in a northerly direction up across the Atlantic, excuse me, the Pacific.
A reminder; we are flying at 32 degrees north and south of the equator on this flight as opposed to the 28 degrees we flew throughout the Gemini program.
We flew 32 degrees in Mercury and we are back at that altitude, at that longitude.
At 1 hour and 8 minutes into the flight, this is Apollo Control in Houston.
transcript.speaker: PAO

tokens: The PAO announcer it getting somewhat tongue-tied with his explanations.
Apollo 7's orbit is inclined 32° to the equator.
In other words, the most northerly and southerly latitude of the spacecraft's ground track will be at 32° north and south respectively.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: Often in instrumentation, the output of a particular sensor is a voltage which bears some sort of relationship with the phenomenon that is being measured.
The Apollo spacecraft is festooned with sensors, each producing a voltage with varying ranges.
An electronics unit takes all these voltage ranges and changes them all to fit within the range 0V to 5V.
This has two advantages.

First, by standardising the range, it is easier to telemeter the signals to Earth as the five-volt range can be represented in a simple digital range, say from binary 00000000 to binary 11111111 (0 to 255 decimal).
Second, instead of filling an already cramped spacecraft with more meters and gauges, a single meter can be used for the less critical readings.
Some simple switching can then permit a large number of sensor outputs to be read on this single meter.
This is the System Test Meter.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: There are two knobs and they act as a simple matrix which allows access to up to 28 different readings.
The crew or Mission Control can translate the voltage reading to a calibrated measurement of, in this case, the pressure of gas inside the compartment where the CM's batteries are installed.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: It is possible for the batteries to outgas under abnormal conditions and this gas enters a common manifold from where it can be manually vented into space.
However, after launch, this manifold is still pressurised with sea-level atmosphere so it is a standard procedure soon after insertion to vent this.
Walt is a Lunar Module Pilot without a Lunar Module, but in the CSM, he is tasked with keeping a close eye on the health of the spacecraft's various systems.
Though Donn is going to operate the vent valve, Walt is guiding him based on his specialist knowledge.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: And I'll watch the meter go right on down there - 1, 2.
You can let it go a little longer.
I can see the meter is still moving.
transcript.speaker: Cunningham
